title: Benchright Industrial Farmhouse Bench
summary: A trestle bench with built-up legs, breadboard-end top, and an optional iron-pipe stretcher for the industrial look. The square legs are the hard part.
description:
A farmhouse-meets-industrial bench: built-up trestle legs, a plank top with breadboard ends, and — optionally — a length of black iron pipe running between the legs as a footrest and design accent. It's the bench version of a popular trestle table, sized to pull up to it.

The legs are the whole challenge. They're assembled from layered, 10°-beveled 1x3 pieces, and getting them square is what makes or breaks the bench. Build them with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, work from the bottom up sandwiching the pieces, and check for square by measuring the diagonals before anything sets — equal diagonals mean a square leg.

The 2x4 aprons set 3/4" in from the leg faces are the main structural supports; screw them with 2-1/2" screws and glue. The 2x2 side rails and breadboard ends follow, cut to fit for the tightest joints. If you want the industrial version, add a 1"-diameter, 60"-long iron pipe between the legs — it's a hardware-store buy, not a woodworking part.

cutList.note: Both ends beveled 10° off square, ends parallel.
Longest point; one end beveled 10° off square.
Short point to short point; both ends 10° off square, NOT parallel.

steps.title: Build the legs square
steps.body: The hardest part of this bench is getting the legs built square. Construct each leg with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, and check for square by measuring the diagonals — equal diagonals mean the leg is true. A racked leg here shows up in every joint above it.

steps.title: Sandwich the leg pieces
steps.body: Working again from the bottom upward, sandwich the remaining leg pieces onto the legs, building them up to full thickness.

steps.title: Attach the aprons
steps.body: Measure 3/4" in from the leg faces and attach the 2x4 aprons with 2-1/2" screws and glue. These are the main structural supports of the bench.

steps.title: Attach the side rails
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 side rails as shown, tying the leg assemblies together along the length.

steps.title: Build the top with breadboard ends
steps.body: Build the top from the 1x6 boards and add the breadboard ends — straightforward at this point. Cut the breadboard ends to fit for the tightest joint.

steps.title: Attach the top and add the pipe
steps.body: Attach the top to the base. Sand and finish. For the industrial version, add the 1"-diameter, 60"-long iron pipe as a stretcher between the legs — a hardware-store part, not a woodworking one.

images.alt: Industrial farmhouse trestle bench with an iron pipe stretcher
